figures.caption: Table 9: Velocity statistics and time-extension factors on Milano (single-disc; multi-disc values in parentheses where they differ substantially). frac over cap reports the fraction of trajectory samples exceeding the 30m/s realistic cap.

Figure 4: Experiment 2: Binary 3:1 density target. (a) Target density: the lower half of the disc has 3× higher density than the upper half, corresponding to a UAV coverage scenario where the southern region has 3× higher service demand. (b) Achieved density from the learned map Gθ (IID evaluation, correlation ρ = 0.79). The sharp boundary at x2 = 0 is smoothed by the continuous transport map, which cannot produce a true discontinuity. Despite this, the global coverage allocation is nearly perfect (75.1%/24.9% vs. target 75%/25%). (c) Allocation accuracy on the 75/25 binary target. Our method achieves 75.1%/24.9% (L1 deviation 0.2 percentage points), a 10× tighter match than the time-warping formula’s 74%/26% (L1 deviation 2.0 pp; Malekzadeh et al. [9], Table I). Jain’s index on demand-normalized service ratios saturates at J ≈ 1.000 for both methods on this 2-region partition and does not discriminate; the L1 allocation distance is the informative metric.

Table 1: Headline metrics on Milano under two no-fly configurations: single-disc (1D) and multi-disc (MD). Mean±std over 3 seeds; best per column among coverage-competitive methods (ρ ≥ 0.5); ‡ achieves low violation at degraded coverage. Full coverage, energy, and NFZ tables in Appendix N.1.

Figure 5: Experiment 3: Constraint flexibility with off-center no-fly zone (NFZ). Three variants on the same Gaussian mixture target (Experiment 1) with an NFZ centered at (0.5,−0.5) (radius 0.2, shown as a circle). (a) Unconstrained: density correlation ρ = 0.89, NFZ violation 0.7%, acceleration ratio 1.77×. (b) NFZ penalty only (λnfz = 50): correlation 0.76, NFZ violation 0.1%, acceleration 1.43×. The NFZ penalty reduces violations by 7× with moderate density cost. (c) NFZ + acceleration penalty (λnfz = 50, λacc = 0.1): correlation 0.66, NFZ violation 0.8%, acceleration 0.93×. Adding acceleration regularization produces trajectories smoother than the latent process, at the cost of further density mismatch.

Figure 8 reproduces the coverage–energy frontier under three additional coverage axes: ℓ1 visitation distance, ℓ2 visitation distance, and Fourier ergodic metric E . These complement the Pearson-ρ view in the main body (Figure 2). The qualitative ordering is preserved across all three: OT-CFM variants trace a Pareto front below all baselines across roughly two decades of the energy axis, with +E at the low-energy end and +NFZ at the highest-fidelity end.

Figure 7: Coverage–NFZ Pareto on Milano. Left: single-disc (1D). Right: multi-disc (MD). Pearson ρ (higher better) plotted against fraction of trajectory inside any NFZ disc (lower better; lower-right is best). Shaded bands are ±1 std over three seeds.

Figure 11: Representative trajectories on the Milano target (purple heatmap) under the multi-disc NFZ configuration (four red dashed circles), one panel per method. The trivial-compliance pattern is even starker here: TOES’s diagonal line happens not to intersect any of the four discs, and the FMEC variants’ corner curls remain far from every NFZ. OT-CFM +NFZ routes around all four exclusion zones while preserving coverage of the surrounding high-density region.

Figure 10: Representative trajectories on the Milano target (purple heatmap) under the single-disc NFZ configuration (red dashed circle), one panel per method. TOES and the FMEC variants fail to redistribute mass across the domain — their zero NFZ violation in Table 8 is accidental. OT-CFM variants traverse the high-density regions while respecting the NFZ; +NFZ visibly bends paths around the exclusion disc, and +E produces visibly straighter and shorter paths consistent with its low-energy-per-meter operating point.
Table 2: Qualitative comparison with existing ergodic trajectory design methods. Quantitative realdata results: §4.2; column definitions, TNFE semantics, and detailed row qualifications: Appendix L.

Figure 2: Coverage–energy Pareto on Milano. Left: single-disc (1D) NFZ. Right: multi-disc (MD) NFZ. Pearson ρ (higher better) plotted against the swept energy budget ∑ ∥v∥2 dt (log scale; lowerright is best); shaded bands are ±1 std over three seeds. The OT-CFM family traces an empirical Pareto front above the evaluated baselines in both configurations; +E occupies the low-energy end and +NFZ the high-fidelity end. The constraint-axis Pareto pattern is summarized in Table 1; full coverage–NFZ figure in Appendix N.2.

Table 10: Per-trajectory online cost and marginal cost of fleet expansion. Baselines report time to produce one trajectory; OT-CFM reports inference time (offline training ≈1.7 h, paid once). For OT-CFM the N=10 fleet cost equals the single-trajectory cost because all agents’ inference passes are independent and run in parallel.

Table 7: Additional coverage and kinematic metrics on Milano under both NFZ configurations. Mean±std over 3 seeds; best per column among coverage-competitive methods (ρ ≥ 0.5). Methods marked † are trivially compliant (cf. Table 1).

Table 8: Full no-fly-zone violation metrics on Milano. Mean±std over 3 seeds; best per column among coverage-competitive methods (ρ ≥ 0.5). Methods marked † are trivially compliant; ‡ achieves low violation at degraded coverage.
